text.title: Test Double Taxonomy
text.summary: The five test-double types: Dummy, Stub, Spy, Mock, and Fake — definitions, when to use each, and selection criteria.

list.item: Dummy — passed but never used. Satisfies a required parameter. Example: null logger passed to a constructor.
Stub — returns pre-configured responses to calls. No behavior verification. Example: always returns {status: 200} for an HTTP client.
Spy — real object that records calls. Behavior is real, but calls can be inspected after. Example: spy on email service to check how many times it was called.
Mock — pre-programmed with expectations. Fails the test if not called as expected. Example: assert payment.charge() called once with correct amount.
Fake — working implementation with shortcuts. Example: in-memory repository instead of real database.

section.title: Selection Guide
section.rule: Use Stub when you only care about the return value, not whether the dependency was called.
Use Mock when you need to verify that a dependency was called with specific arguments.
Use Fake for complex dependencies used heavily across a suite (e.g., a repository interface).
Prefer Fake over Mock for stateful dependencies — Fakes are more maintainable long-term.

section.title: When NOT to Use Mocks
section.rule: Do not mock the system under test itself — test real behavior.
Do not mock value objects or simple data structures — use real ones.
Do not mock what you do not own (third-party libraries) — wrap them in an adapter first, then mock the adapter.
